segment_title: Bartok Pizzicato: Notation and Physical Setup
technique_tags: Bartok pizzicato; notation; snap pizzicato; orchestral effect
use_when: reading Bartok pizzicato notation; identifying the effect
teaching_summary: Identify the Bartok pizzicato symbol, then connect it to the intended snap effect before trying to execute it.
problem_statement: the notation is recognized but not connected to the physical sound effect
common_failure_mode: starting the technique before knowing what the symbol asks for

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
segment_title: Bartok Pizzicato: Finger Placement and Release
technique_tags: Bartok pizzicato; thumb; first finger; controlled release
teaching_summary: Pinch with thumb and first finger, lift only slightly, then release cleanly so the motion stays controlled.
problem_statement: the player cannot produce the snap without losing control of the string
common_failure_mode: pulling with too much hand motion instead of a small controlled release
suggested_experiment: Practice three tiny thumb-and-first-finger lifts, listening for a clean snap without extra motion.
reflection_prompt: Did the string release cleanly without a grab or yank?
notes: CSV timestamp aligns with JSON utterances around 139-152s.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
segment_title: Bartok Pizzicato: Safety and Amount of Lift
technique_tags: Bartok pizzicato; string safety; pitch stability; force calibration
use_when: avoiding excess force; string safety; pitch stability
teaching_summary: Use minimal lift. Excess height can distort pitch and increases the chance of stressing or snapping the string.
problem_statement: the snap is pursued with too much string displacement
common_failure_mode: lifting so far that pitch bends or the string is stressed
suggested_experiment: Compare a large lift with a half-inch-or-less lift and keep the smallest version that speaks.
reflection_prompt: Did the snap speak without pitch distortion or fear of damaging the string?
notes: CSV timestamp aligns with JSON utterances around 152-169s.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
technique_tags: Bartok pizzicato; fingerboard snap; orchestral color; ensemble sound
use_when: ensemble color; fingerboard snap; orchestral special effects
teaching_summary: The point is not individual volume; the controlled release lets the string strike the fingerboard and create a coordinated orchestral slap.
problem_statement: the player treats the effect as a solo volume problem
common_failure_mode: overpulling to be louder instead of producing the coordinated fingerboard snap
suggested_experiment: Play one snap at a moderate size and imagine forty players creating the combined sound.
reflection_prompt: Are you producing the color or just trying to be louder?
notes: Segment overlaps the end of chunk 1 and the reviewed CSV start URL is retained.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
technique_tags: ensemble timing; first violin; lower strings; fermata
use_when: orchestral entrances; lower-string interaction; fermata timing
teaching_summary: Track the exchange between lower strings and first violins. Treat each pizzicato as a timed orchestral response, not an isolated effect.
problem_statement: the entrance is technically correct but not coordinated with the orchestral exchange
common_failure_mode: practicing the pizzicato as an isolated sound instead of a response in time
suggested_experiment: Say lower strings, us, us and lower strings while tapping the sequence before playing.
reflection_prompt: Can you identify who plays before and after your entrance?

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
segment_title: Trills in Texture: Do Not Overplay
technique_tags: trills; orchestral blend; clarinets; dynamic restraint
use_when: the player is over-projecting trills instead of blending with the orchestral texture
teaching_summary: Blend with the woodwinds. Keep the trills present but avoid projecting them as a solo line.
problem_statement: trills are being treated as foreground solo material
common_failure_mode: adding volume and pressure because the motion feels important
suggested_experiment: Play the trill once as a solo line, then once as if matching clarinets under the texture.
reflection_prompt: Does the trill contribute texture without drawing attention away from the ensemble?
notes: JSON chunk 2 content aligns to 210s despite filename offset.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
segment_title: Accent Placement: Front of the Note
use_when: the player is forcing accents or sustaining too much pressure after the attack
teaching_summary: Place energy at the front of the note, then stop pushing. The accent comes from the start, not sustained force.
problem_statement: the accent is heavy because pressure continues after the attack
common_failure_mode: pressing through the whole note instead of releasing after the front edge
suggested_experiment: Play accented notes with a clear start followed by immediate release of extra pressure.
reflection_prompt: Did the beginning speak without carrying tension through the note?
notes: JSON chunk 2 content aligns to 225s despite filename offset.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
segment_title: Measure 6: Controlled Softness After the Tie
technique_tags: tied note; orchestral hierarchy; restraint; dynamic control
use_when: the player needs to subordinate a tied note within a larger orchestral texture
teaching_summary: Underplay the tied note when the surrounding texture carries the musical priority.
problem_statement: a tied note is too prominent in a passage where other material should lead
common_failure_mode: giving every written note equal weight regardless of hierarchy
suggested_experiment: Play the tied note at half the instinctive volume, then listen for the surrounding line in your ear.
reflection_prompt: Can you make that note supportive instead of central?
notes: JSON chunk 2 content aligns to 242s despite filename offset.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
technique_tags: sforzando piano; immediate decay; orchestral balance; accent release
use_when: the player needs a clear attack followed by immediate release into the ensemble
teaching_summary: Make the attack speak, then release immediately into the ensemble rather than sustaining excess pressure.
problem_statement: the sforzando-piano remains loud or pressed after the attack
common_failure_mode: making the sforzando but forgetting the piano decay
suggested_experiment: Play one sforzando-piano with a spoken attack and an exaggerated fast decay.
reflection_prompt: Did the note release into piano right after speaking?
notes: JSON chunk 2 content aligns to 258s despite filename offset.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
technique_tags: bow drop; ricochet; relaxed hand; bow hold
use_when: the player is tense in the bow hand or gripping before a ricochet stroke
teaching_summary: Hover with a natural bow hand. The bow should be supported without gripping or locking the hand.
problem_statement: the setup is already tense before the rebound begins
common_failure_mode: squeezing the bow to prevent dropping it
suggested_experiment: Hover above the string and check that the fingers support the bow without clamping.
reflection_prompt: Could someone gently move the bow without finding a locked hand?
notes: JSON chunk 2 content aligns to 313s despite filename offset.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
segment_title: Bow-Drop / Ricochet: Flat Hair and Tension Diagnosis
technique_tags: bow hair; tension; bow bounce; relaxed arm
use_when: the player has nervous bow bounce, rolled hair, or excess arm tension
teaching_summary: Keep the hair flat and diagnose unstable bounce as a tension problem: gripping the bow and tightening the arm.
problem_statement: the bow bounces from tension rather than useful rebound
common_failure_mode: rolling the hair and tightening the hand or arm when nervous
suggested_experiment: Play a long note with flat hair and deliberately release the thumb and arm before testing the bounce.
reflection_prompt: Is the bounce coming from rebound or from tension in the hand and arm?
notes: This is the target bow bounce alias result.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
segment_title: Bow-Drop / Ricochet: Arm Weight, Not Passive Dropping
technique_tags: arm weight; ricochet initiation; bow drop; sound production
use_when: the player is dropping the bow without enough arm-weight transfer to produce a full stroke
teaching_summary: Use relaxed arm weight rather than merely dropping the bow. The arm travels with the stroke so the contact produces sound.
problem_statement: passive bow drop produces no speaking core
common_failure_mode: letting the bow fall loosely without the arm traveling into the stroke
suggested_experiment: Compare three passive drops against three drops where the relaxed arm travels with the bow.
reflection_prompt: Did the stroke speak immediately without hand tension or forced pressure?
notes: Timing checked against JSON content aligned to 360s and original start URL.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
use_when: the player is getting an unclear rebound because the setup note is too long
teaching_summary: Keep the note before the ricochet extremely short; that setup clears the way for a clean rebound.
problem_statement: the rebound is blurred by the note before it
common_failure_mode: holding the setup note too long and crowding the ricochet
suggested_experiment: Play the pre-ricochet note as a tiny spark, then let the rebound follow.
reflection_prompt: Did shortening the setup note make the ricochet cleaner?
notes: JSON chunk 3 content aligns to 381s despite filename offset.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
technique_tags: cross-string ricochet; arm elevation; G string; D string
use_when: the player is struggling to carry ricochet between G and D strings
teaching_summary: Keep the ricochet concept constant across strings and change arm elevation to match the string level.
problem_statement: the stroke falls apart when moving to another string
common_failure_mode: changing the whole stroke instead of changing arm level
suggested_experiment: Practice the same rebound on G and D while naming only the arm-level change.
reflection_prompt: Did the stroke stay the same while the arm elevation changed?
notes: JSON chunk 3 content aligns to 406s despite filename offset.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
segment_title: Ricochet Across Strings: Preserve the Stroke, Change the Angle
technique_tags: E string; arm angle; transfer learning; ricochet
use_when: the player is reinventing the stroke on each string instead of adjusting geometry
teaching_summary: Do not invent a new stroke for each string. Change arm angle and balance while retaining the same motion.
problem_statement: the player solves every string as a new technical problem
common_failure_mode: abandoning a working rebound when the bow moves to E string
suggested_experiment: Move the same ricochet from G to E and change only the arm angle and balance.
reflection_prompt: Can you preserve the stroke identity across the string change?
notes: JSON chunk 3 content aligns to 435s despite filename offset.

video_title: Tan Dun YouTube Symphony Audition Walkthrough
technique_tags: technique diagnosis; endurance; relaxation; efficient stroke
use_when: the player is over-controlling the stroke or cannot sustain it without fatigue
teaching_summary: Use ease as the test. A sustainable ricochet should feel mechanically light rather than forced.
problem_statement: the stroke works briefly but depends on over-control or effort
common_failure_mode: forcing the rebound until the arm tires
suggested_experiment: Repeat a small ricochet and stop when ease disappears; restart with less grip and less effort.
reflection_prompt: Could you repeat the motion many times without fatigue?
